summary: The author developed a multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) solution for the Lux AI NeurIPS 2024 competition, ultimately placing 14th. Initially experimenting with meta-RL and RNNs to learn hidden relic locations, they pivoted to using a rule-based agent for the first match to reveal fragment positions, which significantly boosted performance. The final approach utilized multi-agent proximal policy optimization (MAPPO) with shared actor parameters, a global critic, and a 47x47 egocentric observation space. Key breakthroughs included revising the reward structure to prioritize match outcomes over point differences and implementing strict action masking to prevent invalid moves.
extensive_summary:
## Initial Approach & Challenges
The team initially aimed to minimize hardcoded rules by exploring meta-reinforcement learning using an RNN-based architecture. They fed 7x7 egocentric observation channels into the RNN, sharing model parameters across agents while maintaining unique inference states. To address information sharing, they experimented with a 24x24 global state observation RNN acting as a central memory unit. However, this approach struggled with wiggling behavior and failed to learn combat strategies due to the lack of exact fragment positions.

## Pivot & Breakthrough
Recognizing the limitations of pure RL for hidden information, they integrated an open-source rule-based agent for the first match to reveal fragment locations, feeding ground-truth positions to the RL agents from match 2 onward. This yielded a massive score jump (from ~200 to 400 points per match). Later, they abandoned the rule-based agent and switched to a 47x47 egocentric observation space centered on each agent. Despite 75% of the observation being NULL, this configuration miraculously improved leaderboard standing from 150+ to 11-15 after a day of training.

## Final Architecture & Training
The final solution employs multi-agent proximal policy optimization (MAPPO) with shared actor parameters across all agents and a global critic that observes both teams without fog. Training ran for 15 days on a single RTX 4090 GPU, accumulating over 30 million update steps and 1 billion environment steps. The team initially used a total point difference reward, which failed to incentivize strategic sacrifices, so they revised it to heavily weigh match win/loss outcomes. They also implemented strict action masking to prevent invalid moves like sapping without sufficient energy.

## Key Learnings & Limitations
The author notes that basic action masking was insufficient; confident masking rules must be applied from the start. Vision Transformers showed promise but were computationally prohibitive, so they settled on a CNN with residual connections. Agent coordination remained a weakness, particularly regarding sapping actions, which required action sampling rather than greedy selection. The team also realized the importance of comprehensive performance metrics and monitoring from day one.
competition_flow: The pipeline uses a rule-based agent for the first match to reveal hidden relic locations, then switches to a multi-agent PPO policy with shared actors and a global critic that takes 47x47 egocentric observations, applies strict action masking, and optimizes for match win/loss rewards over 15 days of GPU training.
data_processing: 47x47 egocentric observation space centered on each agent
Strict action masking for invalid moves (e.g., sapping without energy)
Reward structure revised to weigh match win/loss outcomes over raw point differences
insights: Strict action masking must be implemented from the start rather than expecting agents to learn invalid move avoidance.
Reward structures should prioritize match win/loss outcomes over raw point differences to encourage strategic sacrifices.
Comprehensive performance metrics and monitoring should be implemented before training begins to track behavioral evolution.
Vision Transformers show promise but may be computationally prohibitive compared to CNNs with residual connections under tight budgets.
critical_findings: Using a 47x47 egocentric observation space with 75% NULL values surprisingly improved leaderboard standing from 150+ to 11-15 after minimal training.
Greedy action selection severely hurt performance; action sampling was necessary to encourage agents to explore sapping and coordination.
Feeding ground-truth fragment locations from match 2 onward doubled the mean points collected per match compared to pure RNN approaches.
what_did_not_work: Pure meta-RL with RNNs failed to learn exact fragment positions and combat behavior due to hidden information.
Using total point difference as the reward structure caused agents to fail to learn strategic sacrifices, as they were rewarded for leading even when ultimately losing.
Basic action masking was insufficient; agents failed to learn to avoid invalid moves like sapping without energy unless explicitly masked.
Vision Transformers were too slow to train within the competition's time and compute constraints.

papers.title: Rl $^ 2$: Fast reinforcement learning via slow reinforcement learning.
XLand-minigrid: Scalable meta-reinforcement learning environments in JAX.
A closer look at invalid action masking in policy gradient algorithms.
The surprising effectiveness of ppo in cooperative multi-agent games.
